task: I’ve got to roll out a small process change to my team and I want the message to be clear without turning into a big speech. Can you help me draft something I could send in a quick note, plus a shorter version I could use if I need to say it out loud?

prefs.explanation: He is driven by results and status, so he likely prefers help that gets to the point quickly. A softened or overly diplomatic style would probably feel inefficient to him. He would respond better to direct wording that makes the action or issue immediately clear.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: His work style is efficiency-focused, and he likely values responses that save time rather than unpack everything. Long-winded help would feel like drag unless the extra detail clearly affects the outcome. He probably wants the shortest answer that still lets him act decisively.

prefs.short: Likes responses in clean bullet points or checklists
prefs.explanation: He seems to prefer information that can be scanned and used fast, especially in a managerial setting. A structured format fits his control-oriented style and reduces ambiguity. He would likely dislike rambling prose when a simple checklist would do.

prefs.short: Can tolerate firm pushback if it protects results
prefs.explanation: He is not looking for emotional cushioning, so a direct warning about risk would likely be acceptable if it is practical. Because he has already been burned by compliance problems, he may respect pushback that prevents mistakes. He would still resist pushback that feels like moralizing or slows execution without clear benefit.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English over fancy wording
prefs.explanation: He seems likely to value clear, practical language that reads like business communication rather than polished rhetoric. Jargon is fine only if it is useful and common in the setting. Overly formal or literary phrasing would probably feel unnecessary to him.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it short and direct.

## Quick note
“Starting next week, we’re making a small change to how we handle this process so we can keep things moving and reduce rework. Please follow the updated steps, keep files complete, and flag anything unclear right away. If something doesn’t line up, stop and ask before it turns into cleanup later.”

## Short spoken version
“Starting next week, we’re using the updated process. Follow the steps, keep files complete, and flag issues early so we don’t create rework.”

If you want, I can make it sound:
- firmer
- more branch-specific
- more conversational

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: “keep it short and direct,” “Starting next week,” “stop and ask before it turns into cleanup later.”
Concise throughout; gives a quick note and an even shorter spoken version without extra explanation.
Uses clean structure with headings and bullets: “Quick note,” “Short spoken version,” plus a short option list.
Includes firm pushback on risk: “stop and ask before it turns into cleanup later.” It’s practical, not moralizing, though not especially forceful.
Plain English, simple business language, no fancy wording or jargon beyond common process terms like “rework” and “updated process.”

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here are tighter versions with more authority.

## Quick note
“Starting next week, we’re moving to the updated process. This is now the standard, and I expect everyone to follow it exactly so we keep files accurate and turnaround on track. Make sure the file is complete before it moves, and flag anything unclear right away. If it doesn’t line up, stop and fix it before it leaves your desk.”

## Direct spoken version
“Starting next week, this is the process. Follow the steps, keep the file complete, and flag issues right away so we stay accurate and keep turnaround moving.”

## Firmer version
“Starting next week, we’re using the updated process as the standard. I expect it followed the same way every time so we stay accurate, keep turnaround moving, and avoid problems later. If something is off, stop and address it before it goes any further.”

If you want, I can make these even more:
- branch-manager blunt
- polished for email
- short enough for a huddle opener

per_pref.reason: Very direct and blunt: “This is now the standard,” “I expect everyone to follow it exactly,” and “stop and fix it before it leaves your desk.”
Keeps it concise and action-focused, with short versions and no long explanation. The response explicitly offers “tighter versions.”
Uses clean structure with headings and bullets: “Quick note,” “Direct spoken version,” “Firmer version,” plus a short bullet list of options.
Includes firm pushback and risk control language like “stop and fix it” and “avoid problems later,” which fits practical resistance. It does not really moralize or soften the warning.
Uses plain English and practical wording throughout: “updated process,” “keep files accurate,” “flag anything unclear right away,” with no fancy or literary phrasing.
